figures.gloss: The traditional Korean Lord of Heaven, radically reconceptualized by Donghak and subsequent New Religious Movements. Rather than a transcendent sovereign ruling from above, Hanullim is the ultimate unified life-force of the cosmos, dwelling completely within the human heart.
figures.facet: Dismantles the hierarchical, externalized God of Western monotheism. To interact with Hanullim, one does not look up to the sky, but inward into one's own mind, and outward to the reverent treatment of other humans.
figures.provenance: Choe Je-u (1880) 'Donggyeong Daejeon'; Son Byeong-hi's early 20th-century sermons.

figures.gloss: An impoverished nineteenth-century aristocrat who received a shattering divine mandate from Hanullim in 1860. He was given a spiritual talisman and a cosmic incantation to cure humanity's 'mysterious disease' and found Donghak (Eastern Learning) before being executed for subversion.
figures.facet: He embodies the collision of the meticulous Confucian scholar and the ecstatic, trembling Korean mudang. His revelation was explicitly political—a divine shield forged to protect the local spiritual ecology from Western imperialism.
figures.provenance: Choe Je-u (1881) 'Yongdam Yusa'; Don Baker (2008) 'Korean Spirituality'.

figures.gloss: The supreme philosophical distillation of Cheondoism, declaring absolute ontological equality: because every person literally bears God within them, humanity and Heaven are one and the same entity.
figures.facet: It weaponizes mysticism for radical social equality. If a peasant is literally Heaven, then the neo-Confucian caste system is not just unjust, but an active blasphemy against the cosmic order.
figures.provenance: Son Byeong-hi's doctrinal declarations (1905); Carl Young (2014) 'Eastern Learning and the Heavenly Way'.

figures.gloss: The active state of 'serving Heaven within oneself,' achieved through the rhythmic chanting of a twenty-one-character incantation. It physically aligns the practitioner's vital energy (gi) with the life-force of the universe.
figures.facet: Salvation is not mere belief, but a biological and energetic tuning. By reciting the incantation, the physical body becomes the localized temple where the cosmic Lord wakes up.

figures.gloss: The imminent, radical paradigm shift from the oppressive 'Early Heaven' of hierarchy and conflict to the 'Later Heaven' of mutual beneficence. It is a cosmic springtime, ending the winter of human history.
figures.facet: Unlike apocalyptic destruction myths, Gaebyeok is an evolutionary turning point driven by the collective awakening of humanity. The new heaven and earth are built continuously in this physical world.
figures.provenance: Prominent in both Donghak writings and Kang Il-sun's Daesoon Jillihoe theology.

figures.gloss: The founder of Jeungsanism, who declared himself the Supreme Ruler of the Ninth Heaven incarnated on earth. Between 1901 and 1909, he performed 'Cheonji Gongsa' (Work of Renewing Heaven and Earth) through elaborate shamanic rituals to rewrite the world's destiny.
figures.facet: A deity who walks the earth not merely to preach, but to literally 'rewrite the code' of the universe through sympathetic magic, resolving the accumulated grudges of the dead to avert global annihilation.

figures.gloss: The core tenet of Jeungsanism. The belief that human suffering stems from 'won'—the accumulated grudges and bitterness of historically oppressed humans and spirits. The cosmos cannot evolve until these are pacified.
figures.facet: Frames cosmic salvation as a fundamentally historical and psychological healing process. The divine cannot simply decree peace; it must actively unknot the angry ghosts of history before paradise can emerge.
figures.provenance: Kang Il-sun's teachings; Daesoon Jillihoe texts.

figures.gloss: A talisman received by Su-un in his initial revelation. Drawn on paper, burned, mixed with water, and consumed, it acts as a divine medicine to cure physical illness and align the human body with cosmic energy.
figures.facet: Materializes spiritual salvation as a literal, ingestible medicine. It merges Daoist talismanic healing with the radical egalitarian promise of peasant liberation from disease and political oppression.

figures.gloss: The second patriarch of Donghak, who took leadership after Su-un's execution in 1864 and kept the movement alive underground for some 35 years while hunted by the state. He systematized the teaching into 'sain yeocheon' (treat people as Heaven) and extended reverence to all things, not humans alone, before his own execution in 1898.
figures.facet: He converts a founder's revelation into an organized, survivable institution — doctrine and fugitive leadership fused into one act of transmission across a generation of persecution.
figures.provenance: Choe Je-u's teachings compiled by Haewol into 'Donggyeong Daejeon' (1880); Carl Young (2014) 'Eastern Learning and the Heavenly Way'; Don Baker (2008) 'Korean Spirituality'.

figures.gloss: The third patriarch, who renamed Donghak to Cheondogyo in 1905 to secure it against colonial-era suppression and gave In-nae-cheon its definitive doctrinal formulation. He stood first among the 33 signatories of the March First 1919 Declaration of Independence.
figures.facet: He turns an inward mystical claim into a public political fact — the same doctrine that dissolves caste distinction is marshaled, in his hands, as the theological ground of national self-determination.
figures.provenance: Son Byeong-hi's doctrinal declarations (1905); Carl Young (2014) 'Eastern Learning and the Heavenly Way'.

figures.gloss: Known as the 'Green Pea General' (Nokdu Janggun) for his small stature, he led the Donghak peasant armies of the 1894 Donghak Peasant Revolution against corrupt local officials and Japanese encroachment, organizing tens of thousands under the movement's banner before his capture and execution in 1895.
figures.facet: He is the point where a religious doctrine of human dignity becomes an armed peasant uprising — the moment Donghak's theology of equality is tested against the state's soldiers rather than argued in a text.
figures.provenance: Historical accounts of the 1894 Donghak Peasant Revolution; Carl Young (2014) 'Eastern Learning and the Heavenly Way'.

figures.gloss: The 'Comprehensive Anthology of Eastern Scripture' — Su-un's writings in literary Chinese, compiled and edited by his successor Haewol in 1880, forming the doctrinal core of the Donghak/Cheondogyo canon.
figures.facet: It is the scholarly, literate half of the canon — Su-un's revelation rendered in the classical register that carried authority among the yangban class he came from.
figures.provenance: Choe Je-u, compiled by Choe Si-hyeong (1880); Don Baker (2008) 'Korean Spirituality'.

figures.gloss: Su-un's hymns of 1881, composed in Korean gasa verse rather than literary Chinese, carrying the Donghak teaching to non-literati audiences — the demotic companion to the Donggyeong Daejeon.
figures.facet: Where its companion scripture speaks in the classical idiom of the educated, this text speaks in the vernacular meter ordinary people already sang — doctrine delivered as folk hymn rather than treatise.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The Supreme Lord directly gives rise to the philosophical realization that all humans are identical to Him.
Intra-tradition: The visionary prophet and the literal, physical medicine of salvation he was divinely tasked to distribute.
Intra-tradition: The cosmic dawn of the Later Heaven cannot occur until historical grievances are ritually resolved.
Intra-tradition: The incarnated ruler whose central earthly work was performing the rituals to unknot the world's accumulated bitterness.
Intra-tradition: The rhythmic incantatory practice that physically localizes the cosmic Lord inside the human practitioner's body.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Donghak deliberately used the traditional East Asian name for the Supreme Ruler (Sangje) to localize its distinctly immanent theology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cheondoism formed explicitly in opposition to Western Catholicism, rejecting a distant, judging Creator in favor of an intimately internal divine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme cosmic force that is not a localized deity but the pervasive, immanent energy moving through all things.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conviction that the core of the human being is entirely and radically composed of the highest divine substance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Instead of dissolving the self into emptiness, Cheondoism elevates the human self to the absolute ontological status of Heaven.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The modern Cheondoist codification of the ancient Korean mythic ideal: living in a way that broadly benefits all of humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The definitive, final transformation of the cosmos from an era of conflict and suffering into a perfected epoch of harmony.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A mechanistic, inevitable turning of the cosmic epochs that reshapes the fundamental nature of the physical and moral universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A theological system where the unresolved grievances of the ancestors actively block the prosperity and spiritual progress of the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Recognizes that cosmic and communal health requires actively pacifying the spirits of the dead who suffered injustice.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The aristocratic founder experienced his revelation through the trembling, ecstatic trance traditionally associated with female Korean shamans.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme deity incarnated in a specific historical moment as a human to perform a cosmic intervention that permanently alters reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Beings who physically walk the earth performing precise, structural work to reorganize and repair a flawed cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Using rhythmic, communal somatic practice to awaken a dormant, boiling spiritual energy directly inside the body.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A tangible, created artifact that serves as an engine of healing, abundance, and cosmic realignment for the people.
World-renewal eschatology in two East Asian new religions: the demolition-and-rebuilding of the world and the Great Opening both announce a total cosmic reconstruction vindicating the downtrodden.

Near-simultaneous founder revelations in East Asia (1859 and 1860): a rural man's encounter with the supreme deity revealed as intimately present, launching a mediating new religion amid late-dynastic crisis — parallel emergence, no contact claimed.
The same historical person: Kang Il-sun, sketched in the Korean-NRM cluster shard, rendered here in his own tradition — identity, not analogy.
The identical doctrine carried in both shards — the Korean-NRM cluster holds the summary, this shard the tradition it belongs to.
The same Great Opening concept moving through Korea's founder-religion wave from Donghak into Jeungsanism; shared term and shared expectation, though Kang's sources also include Jeong Yeok cosmology.

Attested succession claim: Jeungsanist doctrine holds Sangje first commissioned Choe Je-u, and descended in person as Kang when Donghak failed — Kang's ministry began in Gobu, the very district where the 1894 Donghak revolution broke out.
Twin central incantations of the same Korean wave: a short, rhythmically chanted formula as the lay believer's whole portable practice — Donghak's 21-character sicheonju and Jeungsanism's Taeeulju.
Shared vocabulary of the same wave: the Great Opening that Donghak awaited and Jeungsanism declared accomplished becomes, in Won Buddhism, a program of spiritual modernization.
Intra-tradition: founder and the successor who preserved his teaching underground for 35 years and compiled it into scripture.
Intra-tradition: third patriarch succeeding the second in the Donghak/Cheondogyo lineage of authority.
Intra-tradition: the leader whose 1905 declarations gave the doctrine of humanity-as-Heaven its definitive, codified form.
Intra-tradition: the peasant-army general operated within the Donghak organizational network under the second patriarch's (eventually joined) authority during the 1894 revolution.
Intra-tradition: the scripture's authored content is Su-un's own revelation writings.
Intra-tradition: compiled and edited into its final anthology form by the second patriarch in 1880.
Intra-tradition: Su-un's own vernacular hymns, the demotic counterpart to his classical-Chinese scripture.

dossier.essence: Korea's first organized indigenous new religion: Donghak ('Eastern Learning'), founded in 1860 when the impoverished rural aristocrat Choe Je-u (Su-un) received a revelation from Hanullim, the Lord of Heaven; he was executed for heterodoxy in 1864. Renamed Cheondogyo ('Religion of the Heavenly Way') in 1905 under Son Byeong-hui, it teaches that Heaven dwells within every person, fueled the 1894 Donghak Peasant Revolution, and supplied leading figures of the 1919 March First independence movement. Today it is small in South Korea and nominally represented in the North by the Chondoist Chongu Party; grouped with it here is the related Jeungsan family of movements (Daesoon Jinrihoe, Jeung San Do) descending from Kang Il-sun.
dossier.cosmos: Hanullim is not a transcendent monarch apart from the world but the ultimate energy (jigi) pervading it: every person 'bears the Heavenly Lord' (sicheonju), so humanity and Heaven are ontologically one (in-nae-cheon). History stands at the Gaebyeok, the Great Opening, in which the exhausted 'Early Heaven' of hierarchy and conflict gives way to a 'Later Heaven' of mutual flourishing — a transformation to be realized in this world as an earthly paradise rather than in a separate hereafter. In the Jeungsan stream, Kang Il-sun (1871–1909) taught that the cosmic order itself required his ritual 'reordering work' (cheonji gongsa) and the resolution of accumulated grievances (haewon sangsaeng) before the new age can open.
dossier.practice: Cheondogyo prescribes five devotions (ogwan): the twenty-one-syllable incantation (jumun), the offering of a bowl of pure water (cheongsu), weekly Sunday assembly (siil), setting aside 'sincerity rice' (seongmi) as offering, and prayer (gido). Its ethic converts the theology directly into conduct — 'treat people as Heaven' (sa-in-yeo-cheon) — which historically grounded anti-caste and anti-colonial activism. The Jeungsan organizations maintain their own devotional systems of incantation and ritual centered on Kang's works.
dossier.canon: Choe Je-u's 'Donggyeong Daejeon' (Great Compendium of Eastern Scripture, in literary Chinese) and 'Yongdam Yusa' (Songs of Yongdam, vernacular Korean verse), published posthumously in 1880–81 by his successor Choe Si-hyeong; the Jeungsan bodies canonize collections of Kang Il-sun's acts and sayings, such as Daesoon Jinrihoe's 'Jeon-gyeong'.
dossier.reflects: Mysticism converted directly into social equality — the divinization of the ordinary person deployed as an argument against caste, empire, and hierarchy.
sources: Carl F. Young, 'Eastern Learning and the Heavenly Way: The Tonghak and Ch'ondogyo Movements and the Twilight of Korean Independence' (2014)
George L. Kallander, 'Salvation through Dissent: Tonghak Heterodoxy and Early Modern Korea' (2013)
Benjamin B. Weems, 'Reform, Rebellion, and the Heavenly Way' (1964)
